The UnitTest standard signature for `setUp` seems to be be just `setUp(self)` per [unittest docs](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/unittest.html#unittest.TestCase.setUp).  One workaround is to implement `other_config()` method which will just return an empty dict in the parent class but can be overridden in `TestViewsWithBootstrap` to inject the required parameters.

I built up a concrete example in which it could fail.
With ``pyramid<1.8`` WebOb dependency is restricted to [``WebOb>=1.3.1``](https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/blob/1.7.4/setup.py#L43), so if we're using an _old_ pyramid version like 1.5 as defined in ``tox`` we have no dependency restriction to ``WebOb>=1.7`` so it is possible to have ``pyramid==1.5,WebOb==1.3.1`` which now will raise ``AttributeError: You cannot access Response.text unless charset is set`` if charset is not defined (if won't happen with ``WebOB>=1.7.1``).

More reference on: [pyramid_swagger/test-fail-webob](https://github.com/macisamuele/pyramid_swagger/tree/test-fail-webob) and [travis build #39](https://travis-ci.org/macisamuele/pyramid_swagger/builds/208413993).

Said that I would enforce the usage of a newer ``WebOb`` dependency. An other possibility is to enforce the use of a newer ``pyramid`` version (which pins up ``WebOb``), but I think that it would be "too much".

Agree. But I don't want to suggest wait_to_read as the recommended way to measure time taken by operations. While it might work for toy problems like this, 
- it is harder to use to measure execution time of multiple operations (requires wait_to_read both before and after the measured operation in multiple places). 
- It is hard to use to measure running time of a block inside a sequence (which is common)
- it won't work for hybrid networks.

The goal of this tutorial is to point people to a recommended way of profiling that works for almost all cases.

However I can add a note along the lines of "While it is possible to use `wait_to_read()` before and after an operation to get running time of an operation, it is not a scalable method to measure running time of multiple operations, especially in a Sequential or Hybrid network"

I'm wondering it this will always be possible and I think that it is not.

At the moment you're customising the value of `args` in case of an `HTTPError`; what happen if the first exception in `exceptions_to_catch` needs additional arguments to be instantiated?
An example could be [`aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientResponseError`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/965ddc1ea3a8fbf1f8d0f64efa0f117a8ebb64ab/aiohttp/client_exceptions.py#L43)

Other than potential issues in creating the exception to raise this could have negative effects over the monitoring systems that we could set as we'll not be able to distinguish if the exception was a "real" exception or an injected one.

To workaroud those issues I would suggest to define a custom exception (ie. `DegradedResponseError`), raise it and ensure that only that exception will be catched.
This allows you to:
 * not have to care about the initialisation of the exception instance (as it will be under complete bravado control)
 * be able to discriminate on the logs what exception have been raised (so monitoring systems will not alert the teams in case of a flood of requests which forces force_degradation)

The original expression is a little confusing, and iterating over the sequence again is a bit unclear. It took me a while to walk through the intent of this code, mostly because of what already existed though. Do you think we could pull this into another helper function? something like:

```python
def _process_columns(columns):
    """Process the dshapes of the columns of a table.

    Parameters
    ----------
    columns : iterable[column]
        The columns to process.

    Yields
    ------
    record_entry : tuple[str, dshape]
        A record entry containing the name and type of each column.
    """
    for col in columns:
        (name, dtype), = discover(col).fields
        yield str(name), dtype
```
here fields replaces parameters[0] because we know this should be a record type.

We can then replace this call site with something like:

```python
record = list(_process_columns(t.columns))
```

First, let me share the reaction of a person who was learning certbot from scratch:  "lineage" is a rather confusing term.
(I saw it being discussed in one of the recent threads here, and I agree.)  It is _sort of_ understandable, but I would have used "set", "family", or, possibly wouldn't not introduce it at all. I would rather think of different versions of the same certificate, calling it collectively a "certificate".
(For comparison: you don't call the collection of different versions of the same file in the source control a _lineage_, right?) 


My biggest confusion is what is the difference between _`lineage`_ and `Certificate Name`. 
Can someone please explain the [practical] difference? So far, from the practical point of view, _Certificate Name_ is used to label the _lineage_ (and its components). If you are intending to keep "lineage" and describe it, I would suggest incorporating that explanation in this document.

Second, I think including the "additional information that Certbot uses to maintain the configuration of your webserver from renewal to renewal" into the notion of lineage is confusing. It is like claiming that birth certificates and county records of birth, marriage and death are a part of your family. I would call those lineage (or, more exactly certificate) configuration.

Yet another suggestion: While a big focus of certbot (and LetsEncrypt) is at the certificates for websites, these certificates are also used for other servers (IMAP/POP3, MTA/MSA, ... ). I think the documentation should be talking more generally about "servers" (as opposed to webservers).
